statement.markdown: You are given an integer array `nums` and an integer `n`. The task is to return `true` if the array can be represented as a stack, and `false` otherwise. An array can be represented as a stack if all elements in the array are in their correct positions. A correct position of an element is when it is at the top of the stack or it is placed on top of all the smaller elements.
statement.inputFormat: The input will consist of two integers `n` and then followed by `n` integers representing the array `nums`. The first integer is the size of the array, and the second line contains the elements of the array separated by a space.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be a boolean value `true` or `false`.
statement.notes: This problem is essentially a validity check for a stack.

examples.explanation: The elements in the array are in their correct positions.
The elements in the array are not in their correct positions.

hints.text: Check if the array can be represented as a stack by comparing each element with its previous elements.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: Check if the array is a valid stack by checking if all elements are in their correct positions.
editorial.bruteForce: Compare each element in the array with all its previous elements to check if it's in the correct position.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a stack to push elements and then check if the popped element matches the current element.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a stack to push elements and then check if the popped element matches the current element. If not, return false. If all elements are popped successfully, return true.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Proof: An array can be represented as a stack if and only if all elements are in their correct positions.
editorial.dryRun: Dry run the optimal solution using the test cases.
commonMistakes: Not checking if all elements are in their correct positions
Not using a stack to pop elements

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& nums); };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean solve(int[] nums); }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, nums: list[int]) -> bool: pass
starterCode.javascript: function solve(nums) { /* your solution here */ } return solve;

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& nums) { stack<int> s; for (int num : nums) { s.push(num); if (s.size() > 1 && s.top() > num) return false; } return true; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean solve(int[] nums) { Stack<Integer> s = new Stack<>(); for (int num : nums) { s.push(num); if (s.size() > 1 && (int) s.peek() > num) return false; } return true; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, nums: list[int]) -> bool: stack = [] for num in nums: stack.append(num) if len(stack) > 1 and stack[-1] > num: return False return True
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function solve(nums) { let stack = []; for (let num of nums) { stack.push(num); if (stack.length > 1 && stack[stack.length - 1] > num) return false; } return true; } return solve;

solutions.python: class Solution: def solve(self, nums: list[int]) -> bool: stack = [] for i, num in enumerate(nums): stack.append(num) for j in range(i, -1, -1): if stack[j] > num: return False return True
solutions.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& nums) { stack<int> s; for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); i++) { s.push(nums[i]); for (int j = i; j >= 0; j--) { if (s.top() > nums[j]) return false; s.pop(); } } return true; } };
